headline: Delivery Rider's Poetry Wins China's Highest Literary Honor
meta_description: Wang Jibing, a 56-year-old food delivery rider, has won the Lu Xun Literary Prize for his poetry collection about gig economy workers.
summary_short: A food delivery rider in China has won one of the country's most prestigious literary prizes for poems written between deliveries.
body:
Wang Jibing never finished school. The 56-year-old from Jiangsu province left as a teenager due to financial hardship, spending decades in precarious work—construction, waste scavenging, street vending—before becoming a food delivery rider around 2019.

Yet his love of reading never wavered. Over the last decade, Wang quietly posted poems and essays on social media, drawing attention for verses that captured the hidden struggles of gig economy workers. In 2022, his poem "Man in a Hurry"—inspired by a frustrating delivery to a wrong address—went viral online.

Now, his second collection, *Low Flight*, has won the Lu Xun Literary Prize, one of China's highest literary honours. The award recognises poetry drawn from interviews with around 200 delivery riders, offering readers a rare glimpse into the pressures they face daily.

"Who says spreading your wings means flying high? Flying low is also flying," reads one line.

Wang's victory reflects a broader shift in Chinese literature: blue-collar workers are increasingly gaining recognition for their authentic voices. Other recent bestsellers include *I Deliver Parcels in Beijing*, a 2023 memoir that captured readers worldwide.

Despite winning the prize, Wang says he has no plans to leave delivery work. "I want a down-to-earth life," he explained, "and the award will never change who I am."
